data.title: Are Prediction Markets Really A Threat To DraftKings?
data.description:
Over the last year or so, prediction markets have gained attention as a potential alternative to traditional sportsbooks.

Smooth onboarding processes, a wide variety of markets, and availability in all 50 states have attracted customers of all stripes, and the growth in top exchanges like Kalshi and Polymarket has been immense.

Naturally, this has sportsbook investors worried, and stocks in top betting companies like Flutter Entertainment (FLUT) and DraftKings (DKNG) have fallen in recent quarters.

At first glance, these new products appear disruptive, but as I'll lay out in this article today, I don’t believe prediction markets are a material threat to the long-term health and viability of sportsbook businesses.

As such, DraftKings - a stock that has been punished over the last year - appears unbelievably well priced for future growth.

I've covered the company twice before here on Seeking Alpha, but I believe the value proposition here is stronger than ever. As a result, I'm reiterating my 'Strong Buy' rating.

Today, I’ll make the case that prediction markets are not a material threat to DraftKings and explain why I see DKNG as an excellent opportunity in the present market landscape.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

The Prediction Market Threat

If you’ve been tuned into the news flow around betting companies over the last year, you’ve likely heard about prediction markets and the threat they pose to traditional sportsbooks.

With simple yes/no event contracts, quick payouts, simple onboarding, and fewer regulatory hurdles, event contract platforms like Kalshi have quickly built a serious book of business.

Not only that, but exchanges offer markets where traders can trade against one another. As such, platform revenue and profitability are directly tied to overall betting volumes and not specific customer outcomes. Obviously, this is a less risky business model than traditional sportsbooks, which both offer and make the markets that users place wagers in.

It's no surprise that investors see prediction contracts as a threat.

Recently, on an earnings call, DKNG management had the following to say about prediction markets, commenting on DraftKings’ advantages when it comes to pricing and user experience (emphasis mine):

We have experienced numerous waves of competition in recent years...

By comparison, predictions are structurally limited, lacking the depth and breadth of a sports betting offering...

I think parlays are just challenging with predictions because it's liquidity based. So you can do some prepackaged stuff, and I think that could work. But to be able to really have a parlay offering that looks anything like what you'd find in an online sportsbook just isn't possible...

If you remember, for us, the reason we have higher margins is... because we have a very strong bet mix. Our singles hold is actually nowhere near what our actual hold rate is. Our singles hold is in the mid-single digits. So that's sort of the comparison point if you're thinking about predictions.

Now on predictions, you have both the fees that the exchange is taking as well as the other fees that are collected from the FCM or anyone else in the ecosystem as well as the spreads that the market makers are taking. So there's a lot more mouth[s] to feed in the ecosystem versus, in our case, we are the market maker solely on our online sportsbook... So really, it makes it that we have more margin to play with there...

The other thing is even if there are cases where there's better pricing, promotions don't really work in the same way in predictions as they do in OSB. So we can give back money and help create cool profit boost and other kinds of promotions for customers that engage them, which really isn't something that predictions is doing.

As you can clearly see in these comments, DraftKings management does not see prediction markets as a significant threat due to the fact that platforms like Kalshi offer ostensibly worse pricing and significantly less flexibility when it comes to incentives and unique bet construction.

While the arguments here make sense, I wanted to test live markets to see whether or not the math holds true.

First, I took a look at pricing for the upcoming NFC Championship game.

On DraftKings, betting the Rams moneyline gets you odds of +130, meaning that a $1,000 wager equals a $2,300 payout:

On Kalshi, the market was priced at roughly the same odds, but the payout for a $1,000 trade was $64 less at $2,236. This is due to the fees that the exchange takes, which vary by the size of your trade:

Then I tested a parlay trade on DraftKings and Kalshi.

On DraftKings, a Rams and Patriots moneyline parlay ended up with odds of roughly +219, meaning that a $1,000 wager would yield just shy of $3,200:

Kalshi, to my surprise, did have the ability to place parlays, known as combo trades, and the pricing was actually six dollars ahead of the DraftKings quote:

For reference, these parlays are RFQ products and priced privately by market makers on the Kalshi platform.

Interestingly, the Kalshi combo product was a better experience than DraftKings’ comments would have led me to believe, but taken together, there were no significant pricing advantages associated with placing trades or wagers on prediction markets. If anything, on average, I found that experimenting with a few other markets yielded similar results, with par or better pricing for DraftKings.

Not only that, but core sportsbook engagement drivers like teasers, boosts, and promotions simply do not exist on prediction market platforms, and the way the markets are set up, with Kalshi (for example) as a neutral third party, makes me believe that they will never exist there.

On top of that, many users access prediction markets through third parties like Robinhood (HOOD). On a recent earnings call, CFO Jason Warnick mentioned that prediction markets were tracking toward a $300 million run rate based on October volumes:

Prediction Markets reached that milestone in less than a year. It's our fastest in history, and it's already tracking towards a $300 million run rate based on October volumes. So just really, really going fast.

In other words, prediction market volumes facilitated by brokers are growing rapidly.

The issue here is that Robinhood itself takes a one-cent-per-contract fee when buying and selling, which adds significant costs to users. If you’re currently trading predictions through Robinhood, you’re getting scraped on prices. In this sense, DraftKings CEO Robins is absolutely correct. There are more mouths to feed in the predictions ecosystem, which results in a worse user experience.

Within this context, DraftKings recently acquired Railbird and launched its own 'DraftKings Predictions' product.

You may be wondering why the company would do this if they thought they could offer a better product with better pricing through their own platform. In short, the reality is that many users trade event contracts in places where sports betting is still not legal, which makes the DraftKings Predictions market more of a user acquisition tool for future markets versus a self-cannibalizing business piece.

As a result, when viewed in sum, I do not believe prediction markets offer a materially better experience than traditional sportsbooks like DraftKings.

While the 3.75% APY on Kalshi balances is nice, the high fees, suboptimal pricing, and lack of more complex bet formats and promotions make me think that DraftKings does not face an existential threat from prediction markets.

Business Progress

Outside prediction markets, DraftKings has continued the strong business momentum we’ve seen over the last three years.

In 2025, DraftKings reported all-time highs in hold percentage, net revenue margin, and parlay mix at 10.4%, 6.7%, and 31%, respectively. This shows that customer behaviors are trending in a positive economic direction for the company:

Not only that, but iGaming revenue also continues to grow, reaching all-time highs in Q3 of 2025 at $450 million:

Finally, no digital gaming business can grow without an expanding user base. In Q3 of 2025, the company announced 10.8 million unique customers over the prior 12 months, another all-time high:

With revenue projected to continue moving up and to the right, it’s hard to argue that management is not executing well.

Finally, a landmark ESPN marketing agreement was reached by the company in the last quarter, which I expect will cement DraftKings’ position as a top sports brand and drive efficient customer acquisition:

This ESPN marketing arrangement also helps with nationwide brand awareness, and as the company’s prediction markets product rolls out to users - even in states where traditional sports betting is not yet legal - I’m expecting significant future cross-selling opportunities.

Valuation

This brings me to the valuation. In short, shares currently trade at roughly 30x blended EBITDA and 2.6x sales.

I view these multiples as particularly attractive for a company poised to grow top and bottom line at a 20%+ clip over the next two years and beyond.

On the top line, continued execution on OSB and iGaming and revenue from new prediction markets should boost sales, and on the bottom line, reduced short-term EBITDA will likely give way to higher long-term returns as investments made today in prediction markets, marketing, and infrastructure pay long-term dividends.

If DraftKings can grow revenues at 22% over the next year, as analysts expect, while growing EBITDA by even 25%, then the stock looks rather cheap overall:

DraftKings trades at a long-term sales multiple of roughly 5x, and strong free cash flow growth, inflecting net profitability, and a slight reversion higher in the company’s historic top-line multiple could easily produce mid-20% annualized returns over the next two years - at a minimum.

As such, this is my base case, and my forward price target over the next year is about $44, assuming sales multiple expansion to 3x:

With a growth opportunity like this, I’m reiterating my Strong Buy rating on the company.

Risks

Of course, there are risks associated with an investment in DraftKings, and I see three key concerns worth noting.

First off, DraftKings, for all its considerable market share, is still the number two player in the domestic sports betting market behind competitor FanDuel (owned by FLUT). If Flutter outcompetes DraftKings, then the company could lose share, thus dampening the financial growth story.

Secondly, continued hype around prediction markets may obscure underlying pricing disadvantages and weigh on sentiment. In other words, prediction markets remain a suboptimal option for bettors, but the differences may not be large enough for users to switch back to DraftKings’ vertical model. At the same time, if the narrative in the space still centers on prediction markets one year from now, I would be surprised if DraftKings were able to make any headway on multiple expansion.

Finally, DraftKings’ 30x blended EBITDA multiple remains more expensive than the broader market or competitors like Flutter. Thus, DraftKings’ multiple may compress over the short to medium term, especially in the event of broad market volatility.

Summary

Overall, however, while there are risks, I see DraftKings as a significant growth story that should grow unimpeded by the rise of prediction markets. Internal company metrics reflect ongoing growth and value capture, and the company’s predictions product should shore up any offering weaknesses while giving the company a foothold in regions that have not yet legalized gambling.

Trading at less than three times sales and roughly 30x underlying earnings power, the growth in the business and the potential for moderate multiple expansion should drive 20%+ annualized growth in the stock over the next few years.

Hence, my Strong Buy rating.

Cheers~

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Fed's Favorite Inflation Gauge 'Stuck'?
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Rates climb after core PCE holds at 2.8% YoY. (0:14) GE Aerospace slips as analysts warn margin expansion may slow. (1:15) EU regulators prepare parallel reviews of rival Warner bids. (2:18)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Our top story so far, rates are moving higher after a raft of morning economic data pointing to sticky inflation, solid growth, and a stable labor market.

The core PCE price index — the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge — rose 2.8% year over year in November, unchanged from October and in line with expectations.

Olu Sonola, head of U.S. economic research at Fitch Ratings, says the data suggest inflation is “stuck.”

“PCE isn’t converging back to target, but it’s also not re-accelerating on tariff-driven pressures,” he said, adding that resilient consumers and higher-income wage gains keep the Fed in a holding pattern and policy “restrictive for longer.”

Q3 GDP was revised up to an annualized 4.4% from 4.3%, helped by stronger exports and investment, partly offset by a small downgrade to consumer spending.

And weekly initial jobless claims edged up by 1K to 200K, better than the 209K expected.

Continuing claims dipped to 1.849M from 1.875M the week before.

Among active stocks, GE Aerospace (GE) is lower, even after Q4 adjusted earnings and 2026 guidance topped Wall Street expectations, as analysts flagged signs that its torrid growth may be slowing.

J.P. Morgan analyst Seth Seifman, who rates the stock Overweight, says GE may find it harder to expand margins this year than in 2025, citing headwinds from the 9X engine, equipment growth outpacing services, and the “potential for further spare engine normalization.”

Procter & Gamble (PG) is higher after CFO Andre Schulten said on the earnings call that the company has just completed what it fully expects will be the softest quarter of its fiscal year.

BNP Paribas analyst Kevin Grundy noted that while P&G’s top-line results were muted and sentiment is increasingly tied to improving volume trends, he still sees a high likelihood the company can achieve its EPS guidance.

And Abbott Labs (ABT) is the S&P’s biggest decliner after reporting lower-than-expected Q4 2025 revenue, hurt by underperformance in its Nutrition segment.

Abbott’s projected organic sales growth for 2026, as well as its outlook for Q1 adjusted EPS, also came in below expectations.

In other news of note, European competition watchdogs are set to examine the bids from Netflix (NFLX) and Paramount Skydance (PSKY) for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) at the same time.

Bloomberg reports that parallel reviews are now inevitable, given the timing of the rival proposals and the fact that both sides have already sounded out EU regulators.

Sources say a double probe would give Brussels unusual leverage over Warner’s fate, since it could quickly clear a deal for one bidder while launching a more in-depth investigation into the other.

But whichever company ends up with Warner, there are already a couple of clear winners: cheers are echoing on the deal floors of J.P. Morgan (JPM) and Allen & Company, which stand to earn about $90M each for advising Warner — no matter who wins the bidding war.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Bank of America has updated its US 1 list — its lineup of best ideas among Buy-rated U.S.-listed stocks.

Merck (MRK) is the newest addition, while Apple (AAPL) keeps its spot after passing the mandatory 52-week review.

Other names on the list include Spotify (SPOT), Walmart (WMT), Wells Fargo (WFC), Nvidia (NVDA) and Progressive (PGR).

data.title: Teradyne: The Market Is Ignoring The Cyclicality Of Its Business
data.description:
Teradyne Inc. (TER) is one of the best businesses in the semiconductor supply chain, full stop. It dominates the high-end testing markets, earns very strong margins when the volumes show up, and has a very long history that proves that it can survive cycles better than most.

All of those things are great, but today’s price isn’t. At ~$232/share, I believe the stock is valued as if the current demand backdrop is the new normal, which isn’t the case. That is a dangerous assumption for a business that depends on customer capex timing. I’m not concerned with execution; Teradyne performed well throughout 2025. Demand and margins rose materially, and its earnings profile rebounded.

The issue is that the market has taken those improvements and extrapolated them forward with very little skepticism from shareholders. I’m looking forward to the Q4 2025 results that come out in a few weeks, but even if they meet shareholder expectations, I will be sticking to my Hold position.

Now, I’ll outline what the company actually is and how it makes money before diving into other parts of my thesis.

What Is Teradyne?

Teradyne is a semiconductor test company first. Everything else is secondary. The two main themes around the business are increasing automation and higher semiconductor complexity.

But testing is where the money is made, where the moat is. I’d say it is what drives the stock.

The Semiconductor Test segment sells automated test equipment that chipmakers and outsourced test providers use to validate the increasingly complex chips across compute, networking, automotive, industrial, and power markets. Chips are getting harder to design, package, and qualify, which means that testing is becoming more critical and more expensive. That dynamic is structural, and it is why Teradyne exists.

Product Test sits next to this as a supporting business. It is a much smaller segment that covers system-level and electronics tests outside the core semiconductor flow. It is also steadier, less cyclical, and helpful with the margins, but it does not change the valuation story. I think of it as ballast and not much else.

Then, there is Robotics, which is built around collaborative robots and autonomous mobile robots that are useful in manufacturing and logistics environments. I’d describe it as strategically interesting but financially disappointing, at least for now. Higher interest rates and cautious industrial spending have kept it from contributing meaningfully to growth, and especially the margins, over the last year. I think it will become more important moving forward, but it is not doing much and basically doesn’t matter to Teradyne’s earnings power today.

Teradyne Sits In The Middle Of The Semiconductor Supply Chain

Teradyne’s relevance comes down to one simple fact: testing becomes more valuable as semiconductors become more complex. This has been the case for decades, but it is now becoming more pronounced because of higher performance requirements, packaging complexity, and reliability standards around the newer technologies.

At the high end of semiconductor testing, competition is limited, and the market is effectively an oligopoly with a few key players like Advantest and ASE Technology. Most customers aren’t optimizing for the lowest upfront costs; they want the best quality. So, once a tester platform is qualified and ramped into production, customers do not switch lightly. Yield, throughput, and time-to-market matter far more than the upfront price, and that creates real inertia in Teradyne’s installed base.

That positioning is especially powerful in the current environment. AI-related compute and memory are driving more advanced chips and ecosystems: think Custom ASICs, high-bandwidth memory, advanced packaging, power management, and networking silicon. These elements all raise test intensity, and they require testing that has tighter tolerances and meets higher performance thresholds. These are capabilities that Teradyne already has, so they give it an advantage. I should also point out that this is why Semiconductor Test demand re-accelerated in the second half of 2025 after a digestion phase earlier in the year.

What I do not ignore, though, is the cycle. AI infrastructure buildouts are real, but they still flow through customer capex decisions, and those decisions typically come in waves. Customers can ramp aggressively, which brings higher demand for Teradyne’s business. When they pause to digest that capacity, we also see the slowdown in demand. So, Teradyne isn’t immune to these market forces.

Outside of AI, there are other supportive end markets that provide breadth but not immunity. Automotive, industrial, power, defense, aerospace—these are all in that bracket, and they are less volatile than consumer electronics. But they also aren’t growing fast enough to support the company’s economics on their own. Product Test fits into that smoothing role, but Robotics is still hostage to macro conditions, as I mentioned earlier.

So yes, Teradyne is well-positioned, but well-positioned does not mean non-cyclical, and at today’s valuation, the market is behaving as if it does.

The Numbers In 2025

If you strip away the narrative and just look at the financials, Teradyne had a 2025 that was uneven early, then grew decisively stronger as the year progressed.

Through the first nine months of FY25, the company generated $2.11 billion in revenue. That’s a headline figure, but if you look at H1 first, you’ll see that the company’s topline was dealing with customer digestion, something I mentioned before. H2 is on track to be much better, and I’m tempted to call Q3 an inflection quarter, because the $769 million in revenue came from renewed demand in AI-related compute and memory. I think Q4 will be the main inflection quarter, though, especially if the company manages to hit the $1 billion mark, as it stated in its guidance for the quarter.
