Given that the key difference between any funds is reflected in their Total Return indicators, let's take a look at the difference between them. We will analyze a period of almost two years (from March 6, 2024, the launch date of QDTE), for which QDTE's Total Return is 9.60% higher (40.10%). That said, a 30.50% return for FEPI is still pretty good. Also, for the last year, there's been a really small difference between the funds (QDTE is up 0.57%).

Risks to a "Buy" for FEPI

Given that I have chosen FEPI over QDTE and recommend "Buy" for the former, there is a need to assess the potential risks associated with its maximum concentration of capital in a small number of stocks (15 holdings). Due to this, there is limited diversification in this fund, so if even one company fails, performance (reflected by NAV), can drop significantly. As an example, a 10% drop in one of the stocks in the portfolio would impact NAV potentially in the mid-single digits.

While FEPI is capable of more effectively realizing the growth potential of stocks, it still has a limited maximum return. As a result, even a 5-10% rally in a single day does not allow the fund to capture all possible profits. Furthermore, during a severe bear market, there is no real protection for FEPI against a significant drop in its NAV. While the premium received, paid out as dividends to shareholders, serves as a partial buffer and is limited to the premium income, this is not a real mechanism for protecting against falling prices. As a result, if an AI crisis develops in the market, then the fund's performance will be correspondingly negative.

Conclusion

Therefore, comparative analysis between FEPI and QDTE reveals fundamental differences between them. I consider QDTE to be a more aggressive instrument, ideally suited for generating stable weekly income, yet it may yield adverse results in the long term due to NAV erosion. My recommendation for QDTE is therefore to "Hold," but I recommend "Buy" for FEPI. One thing I like is that this fund is not only betting on Mag 7, as well as semiconductor companies. This fund is suitable for investors like me who believe in the continuation of the AI supercycle in 2026. In addition, it offers a high dividend yield of around 25%, ideal for those who are dividend-oriented and seek to reinvest their payments back into the assets they have purchased.

data.title: Bio-Rad Laboratories Still Hampered By Underwhelming Core Growth
data.description:
Bio-Rad's (BIO) (BIO.B) management has been taking steps to improve its long-term core growth prospects, but it takes time for those steps to translate into real results. Meanwhile, the company has to deal with challenging end-market conditions, including considerable funding uncertainties for academic labs and biotechs, as well as global tariffs.

I wasn’t that bullish on Bio-Rad a year ago, and the shares have seen meaningful volatility since then. Shares sold off sharply in the first half of 2025 on weak results and guidance before rallying around 60%. Net-net, the shares are still down about 15% since my last update, worse than peers/comps like Agilent (A), Bio-Techne (TECH), Qiagen (QGEN), and Thermo Fisher (TMO).

Bio-Rad’s valuation continues to be heavily influenced by its roughly one-third stake in Germany’s Sartorius (SARTF). I could argue for 20% or more upside based on improving end-market conditions, particularly Sartorius’s bioprocessing business, but I still don’t fundamentally like Bio-Rad’s business all that much, and I think investors who want exposure to Sartorius would be better served owning Sartorius. That said, expectations are low, and even relatively modest progress in revenue growth and/or margins could drive re-rating.

Waiting For The End Of A Volatile Year With Volatile Guidance

Bio-Rad investors have had a bit of a rollercoaster ride regarding company guidance this year. While management started off with 2.5% core revenue growth guidance for 2025 (at the midpoint), that number moved to almost zero before rebounding a bit, with adjusted operating margin guidance likewise moving from 13%-13.5% initially to 10%-12% at the worst and then to 12.5%-13.5% as the tariff situation between the U.S. and China evolved.

This leaves a wide spread of potential outcomes for Q4, including potentially mid-single-digit growth at the high end, with the current sell-side average estimate at 4%. Underlying Life Sciences revenue should be up low single digits given recent trends and guidance from other life science tools companies, with some possible upside from end-of-year budget spending on tools like the company’s droplet digital PCR (or ddPCR) instruments. On the other hand, while process chromatography has rebounded this year and should be up double digits again in Q4 (against a roughly 50% year-over-year drop), this has been a tough line item to model.

Diagnostics is likely to see low growth in the quarter. Bio-Rad has been seeing steady, though not particularly spectacular, demand for quality control and immunology products, offset by weaker diabetes testing in China. That headwind should ease in Q4 as the company annualizes that impact, letting the low single-digit underlying growth come through.

Weak guides for margins have set the stage for multiple quarters of EBITDA and operating income beats, but I expect this to be a little better dialed in for the fourth quarter, and while I see some upside, I don’t expect a blowout (I’m looking for around $125M in adjusted EBITDA).

A Better Year Ahead, But Likely Still Pressured

There have been a few positive developments in Bio-Rad’s end markets, but the overall environment is still challenging, and that doesn’t bode well for a return to the 3% to 5% long-term growth range that management has previously targeted.

On the positive side, a federal appeals court recently upheld a ruling that blocks the NIH from capping reimbursement for indirect overhead costs at 15% of grant awards, and these indirect reimbursements are an important source of funding for lab equipment. It remains to be seen what, if anything, the administration does in response. Even so, there’s another budget deadline on the way, and it seems unlikely that there’s much appetite for a meaningful (if any) increase in NIH funding.

While the NIH is a limited part of Bio-Rad’s business (around 4% by management’s estimation) and overall U.S. academic research is less than 10% of revenue, there’s still a trickle-down effect from weaker NIH funding. Likewise, the biopharma funding environment has only recently started to improve from weak levels, and multiple life science tools companies have pointed to weaker spending from this sector as a driver of weaker sales.

More positively, a substantial amount of Bio-Rad’s revenue comes from transfusion, reference, and hospital labs where the spending environment is more stable. It remains to be seen what the elimination of ACA subsidies will do to healthcare utilization (including lab work), but this tends to be a more stable business, and I expect that to remain the case.

Still Waiting For ddPCR To Accelerate

One of the challenges with Bio-Rad from a stock-picking perspective is the lack of meaningful growth drivers. While the company has high-level exposure to growth segments like proteomics, cell biology, and bioproduction, it doesn’t tend to be with high-profile, high-growth instruments or consumables.

The one exception is the company’s droplet digital PCR business. The use case for ddPCR is that it produces more sensitive, more accurate, and more reproducible results faster and more cost-effectively than alternatives like next-gen sequencing or qPCR.

Management recently revised its addressable market here from over $10 billion to around $5 billion, acknowledging that the diagnostics opportunity is still in its very early stages (with significant long-term opportunities in liquid biopsy and residual disease detection). Even with management refocusing on the nearer-term opportunities in life sciences (targeted sequencing and relative quantitation for drug discovery, translational research, and bioprocessing), the market is still likely only around 10% penetrated today, with Bio-Rad enjoying a good initial lead ahead of Qiagen and Thermo Fisher.

Management has also been spending to build up its capabilities here. Bio-Rad spent over $218 million upfront to acquire Stilla Technologies and its lower-ASP ddPCR system. Coupled with its QX Continuum platform, Bio-Rad now has a fuller array of solutions for this market, and I do see it as a double-digit growth opportunity extending over several years—particularly if/when it becomes a core enabling technology for liquid biopsies.

The Outlook

Guidance for better than 2% revenue growth in FY’26 would likely be considered a win by the Street, let alone a return to the more normalized 3%-plus growth rate. The latter could be a big ask in an environment of flat NIH funding and still-lackluster biopharma funding, but a stronger year for process chromatography and strength in ddPCR demand/uptake could drive it.

At this point I’m only looking for around 3% core revenue growth over the next three years, as whatever potential recovery may be present in life sciences is offset by a lack of proprietary core drivers outside of ddPCR. Management has been saying more of the right things lately with respect to ROI-driven R&D allocation decisions, but it takes time for R&D projects to translate into revenue growth, and Bio-Rad has never been particularly good at it—again, this is a company that has grown revenue at an annualized rate of less than 5% over the last 20-plus years.

Bio-Rad’s valuation cannot be separated from the value of its stake in Sartorius, including 38% of ordinary shares and 28% of preferred shares. The value and prospects of Sartorius would be a separate article in its own right, so for now I’ll substitute in the sell-side’s price targets for the underlying shares and note that Sartorius’s bioprocessing business does appear to be turning up again (and the long-term case for healthy growth here remains sound). Net of expected taxes, I value Bio-Rad’s stake at around $171/share.

Between discounted cash flow and multiples-based approaches (EV/EBITDA, EV/revenue, et al.), I believe Bio-Rad is worth around $338 to $390 today, with upside if or when management can drive better sustained revenue growth. Low-to-mid single-digit revenue growth isn’t worth much in life sciences, supporting around 11x EBITDA or 1.5x revenue, but a path to high single-digit growth would at least put a 2x EV/revenue multiple in play (and a $400-plus fair value after including the Sartorius stake).

The Bottom Line

Bio-Rad likely is undervalued today, but I still don’t really like the business. I can appreciate the argument that this is a way to leverage a stake in Sartorius with operational execution upside at Bio-Rad, but I just don’t have that confidence in management at this point, and the overall operating environment is still challenging. It’s not well-covered, so upside could take the Street by surprise and drive some rerating upside, but I need more core growth to get really bullish about this name.
